questions.question: A cycle on n vertices is isomorphic to its complement. The value of n is _____.
questions.to_find: The value of n for which C_n is isomorphic to its complement

understand.plain: A graph G is self-complementary when G and its complement G^c are isomorphic. For a cycle C_n to be self-complementary, G and G^c must have the same number of edges, which forces n(n-1)/4 to be an integer equal to n — giving n = 5. The 5-cycle C_5 is indeed self-complementary and is a well-known example.

keywords.explain: The complement G^c of a graph G on n vertices has the same vertex set; two vertices are adjacent in G^c iff they are NOT adjacent in G.
keywords.example: C_4 complement is 2K_2 (two disjoint edges), not isomorphic to C_4.

keywords.explain: A graph isomorphic to its own complement. It must satisfy |E| = C(n,2)/2 = n(n-1)/4, so n ≡ 0 or 1 (mod 4).
keywords.example: C_5 (5-cycle) and P_4 (path on 4 vertices) are classic self-complementary graphs.

understand.visual_alt: The 5-cycle C_5 shown alongside its complement (a 5-pointed star / pentagram), illustrating that both are isomorphic — C_5 is self-complementary.

given.aim: Find n such that the cycle C_n is isomorphic to its own complement.

terms.meaning: A simple cycle on n vertices with exactly n edges.
terms.connects: The cycle is one of the graphs we test for self-complementarity.

terms.meaning: Graph on the same vertex set where adjacency is flipped: edge iff not in G.
terms.example: G^c of C_5 has C(5,2) - 5 = 5 edges, matching C_5.

terms.meaning: G isomorphic to G^c implies |E(G)| = C(n,2)/2 = n(n-1)/4.
terms.connects: Setting |E(C_n)| = n equal to n(n-1)/4 gives the equation to solve.

given.plan: Set the number of edges in C_n equal to half of C(n,2): n = n(n-1)/4. Solve for n to get n = 5, then verify C_5 is genuinely self-complementary.

steps.title: Edge count condition for self-complementarity
steps.apply: C_n has exactly n edges. For self-complementarity: n = n(n-1)/4.
steps.note: Divide both sides by n (n > 0): 1 = (n-1)/4, so n - 1 = 4, giving n = 5.

steps.title: Verify C_5 is self-complementary
steps.formula_raw: isomorphic graphs share degree sequence and edge count
steps.apply: C_5 has 5 edges. Its complement K_5 minus C_5 also has 10 - 5 = 5 edges, and every vertex still has degree 2 in the complement. A bijection maps C_5 to its complement, confirming isomorphism.
steps.note: n = 5 is the unique cycle length that is self-complementary.

questions.formula_note: The self-complementary edge condition |E| = n(n-1)/4 is the key: setting it equal to n (edges in C_n) directly yields n = 5.

questions.question:
The following simple undirected graph is referred to as the Petersen graph.

[Petersen graph: 10 vertices, 15 edges, 3-regular, with an outer 5-cycle, an inner pentagram, and spokes connecting them.]

Which of the following statements is/are TRUE?

A. The chromatic number of the graph is 3.
B. The graph has a Hamiltonian path.
C. The following graph is isomorphic to the Petersen graph. [A different drawing of the Petersen graph is shown.]
D. The size of the largest independent set of the given graph is 3. (A subset of vertices of a graph form an independent set if no two vertices of the subset are adjacent.)
questions.to_find: Which statements about the Petersen graph are true

understand.plain: The Petersen graph is a famous 3-regular graph on 10 vertices and 15 edges. It is known to have chromatic number 3 (not bipartite, but not 4-chromatic), to possess a Hamiltonian path (though not a Hamiltonian cycle), and to be highly symmetric so many different-looking drawings are isomorphic to it. Its independence number is 4, not 3, making statement D false.

keywords.explain: The minimum number of colours needed to colour every vertex so no two adjacent vertices share a colour.
keywords.example: Petersen graph chi = 3: it contains an odd cycle (5-cycle), ruling out 2, but 3 colours suffice.

keywords.explain: A path that visits every vertex exactly once. Different from a Hamiltonian cycle which must return to the start.
keywords.example: Petersen graph has a Hamiltonian path but famously has no Hamiltonian cycle.

keywords.explain: The size of the largest independent set — a set of vertices with no two adjacent.
keywords.example: In the Petersen graph the independence number alpha = 4, not 3.

understand.visual_alt: Standard drawing of the Petersen graph: outer 5-cycle (pentagon), inner 5-cycle as a star (pentagram), with spokes connecting each outer vertex to the corresponding inner vertex.

given.aim: Determine which statements about the Petersen graph are true.

terms.meaning: A specific 3-regular graph on 10 vertices and 15 edges, girth 5, serving as a standard counterexample in graph theory.
terms.example: It is the smallest 3-regular bridgeless graph with no 3-edge-colouring.
terms.connects: Its known invariants let us evaluate every statement directly.

terms.meaning: Minimum colours for a proper vertex colouring. Petersen graph: chi = 3.
terms.example: It contains 5-cycles (odd), so chi >= 2+1 = 3; three colours suffice.

terms.meaning: Size of largest independent set. For Petersen graph alpha = 4.
terms.example: Pick 4 non-adjacent vertices — a maximum set — to see alpha = 4, disproving D.
terms.connects: Determines that statement D is FALSE.

given.plan: Use the well-known invariants of the Petersen graph (chromatic number 3, Hamiltonian path exists but no Hamiltonian cycle, high symmetry giving many isomorphic drawings, independence number 4) to classify each statement.

steps.title: Statement A — chromatic number 3
steps.formula_raw: Petersen graph: 10V, 15E, 3-regular, chi=3, alpha=4, has Hamiltonian path, no Hamiltonian cycle
steps.apply: The Petersen graph contains odd cycles (girth 5), so chi >= 3. A valid 3-colouring exists. Hence chi = 3. Statement A is TRUE.
steps.note: It is not bipartite (would need chi=2) because it has odd cycles.

steps.title: Statement B — Hamiltonian path
steps.formula_raw: Petersen graph has a Hamiltonian path but no Hamiltonian cycle
steps.apply: It is a known fact that the Petersen graph has a Hamiltonian path (e.g., 1-2-3-4-5-...) even though it has no Hamiltonian cycle. Statement B is TRUE.
steps.note: This is a standard result distinguishing Hamiltonian path from Hamiltonian cycle.

steps.title: Statements C and D
steps.apply: C: The alternate drawing shown is another standard representation of the Petersen graph (all such drawings are isomorphic due to its vertex-transitivity). Statement C is TRUE. D: The independence number alpha(Petersen) = 4, not 3. Statement D is FALSE.
steps.note: Only A, B, and C are TRUE.

questions.formula_note: The stored Petersen graph invariants (chi=3, Hamiltonian path yes, alpha=4) are the key reference; they directly resolve all four statements.

questions.question:
How many perfect matchings are there in a complete graph of 6 vertices?

A. 15
B. 24
C. 30
D. 60
questions.to_find: Number of perfect matchings in K_6

understand.plain: A perfect matching in a graph on n vertices (n even) is a set of n/2 edges that together cover every vertex exactly once. In the complete graph K_{2m} every possible perfect matching exists. The count follows the double-factorial formula: (2m-1)!! = (2m-1) * (2m-3) * ... * 3 * 1. For K_6 (m=3) this gives 5 * 3 * 1 = 15.

keywords.explain: A matching that covers all vertices — each vertex is in exactly one matched edge, so there are n/2 edges total.
keywords.example: In K_4 with vertices {1,2,3,4}: {1-2, 3-4}, {1-3, 2-4}, {1-4, 2-3} — exactly 3 perfect matchings.

keywords.explain: Product of all odd numbers from 1 to (2m-1). Counts perfect matchings in K_{2m}.

understand.visual_alt: K_6 has 6 vertices; its 15 perfect matchings each pair all 6 vertices into 3 disjoint edges. The counting follows the product 5 x 3 x 1 = 15 by choosing a partner for each successive unpaired vertex.

given.aim: Count the number of perfect matchings in the complete graph K_6.

terms.meaning: Complete graph on 6 vertices; every pair of vertices is connected by an edge. Has C(6,2) = 15 edges.
terms.example: Vertices labelled 1 through 6, all 15 edges present.
terms.connects: We need to select 3 non-overlapping edges that together cover all 6 vertices.

terms.meaning: n = 2m so for K_6, m = 3. The formula (2m-1)!! applies.
terms.example: K_4: m=2, matchings = 3!! = 3. K_6: m=3, matchings = 5!! = 15.
terms.connects: Determines which value of the double factorial to compute.
Gives the exact count of perfect matchings in K_{2m}.

given.plan: Apply the formula for perfect matchings of K_{2m}: pick a partner for vertex 1 in (2m-1) ways, then for the next unpaired vertex in (2m-3) ways, and so on; multiply the choices.

steps.title: Apply the perfect matchings formula for K_{2m}
steps.formula_raw: number of perfect matchings of K_{2m} = (2m-1)!! = (2m)! / (2^m * m!)
steps.apply: K_6 has n = 6 = 2*3, so m = 3. Number of perfect matchings = (2*3 - 1)!! = 5!! = 5 * 3 * 1 = 15.
steps.note: Alternatively: vertex 1 can pair with any of 5 others, then the next free vertex pairs with 3, then the last pair is forced: 5 * 3 * 1 = 15.

questions.formula_note: The double-factorial formula (2m-1)!! for perfect matchings of K_{2m} is the key tool; for K_6 it gives 5!! = 15 directly.
